coredata.dc:title: Bridging between NiAl-LDH and g-C3N4 by using carbon quantum dots for highly enhanced photoreduction of CO2 into CO
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Wentao
Wang, Qiu
Liu, Zhi
Ding, Guixiang
coredata.dc:description: A series of treble NiAl-LDH/g-C3N4/carbon quantum dots (LDH/CN/CQDs) photocatalysts is successfully prepared for the photoreduction of CO2 to CO via a facile hydrothermal pathway. In the 3D flower-like LDH/CN/CQDs, CQDs not only achieve the efficient inhibition of charge recombination but also act as the unhindered “electronic bridges” to synergistically construct a classical type-Ⅱ charge transfer configuration, which synchronously permits the effluence of photogenerated electrons from CN to LDH and holes from LDH to CN, and promotes ultraviolet–visible irradiation respondence. The sample of LDH/CN/CQDs-6 is the optimal one amongst the LDH/CN/CQDs with a larger special surface area (98.43 m2g−1) and an appropriate content of CQDs (66.9 wt%), exhibiting the highest CO evolution rate (5.2 μmol·g−1·h−1) under visible light irradiation without any sacrificial agent or photosensitizer in water. This is 26.8- and 20.9-fold higher than those of the pristine LDH, pure CN, and their binary counterparts, respectively, and also outperforms most reported LDH-based photocatalysts. As unhindered electron conduction bridges, the highly dispersed CQDs in the LDH/CN heterojunction significantly increase utilization efficiency of light energy and separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. This work provides a beneficial attempt to integrate CQDs with LDH/CN for the positive synergetic effect on both photoelectric properties and electron transfer to obtain highly enhanced photocatalytic activity of CO2 into CO, and expected to be extended towards broader photocatalytic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NiAl-LDH
g-C3N4
Carbon quantum dots
CO2 photoreduction

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Energy Conversion and Storage and Environmental Technologies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) XRD patterns and (c, d) FTIR spectra of different samples.
TEM images of (a) LDH, (c) CN, (d) LDH/CN, and (e) LDH/CN/CQDs-6, HR-TEM images of (b) LDH and (f) LDH/CN/CQDs-6.
XPS spectra of LDH, CN, LDH/CN, and LDH/CN/CQDs-6: (a) C 1 s, (b) N 1 s, (c) Ni 2p, and (d) Al 2p.
(a) UV–vis DRS profiles and (b) corresponding Tauc plots of different samples.

(a) Time course of CO2 photoreduction with water on different samples, (b) cycling photostability of LDH/CN/CQDs-6, (c) isotope analysis of 13CO for LDH/CN/CQD-6, and (d) influence of reaction temperature on CO yield of LDH/CN/CQDs-6.
(a) Photocurrents, (b) EIS profiles, (c) PL spectra, and (d) TRPL decay spectra of different samples.
Proposed mechanism on photoreduction of CO2 to CO of the LDH/CN/CQDs-6.

head.ce:title: Bridging between NiAl-LDH and g-C3N4 by using carbon quantum dots for highly enhanced photoreduction of CO2 into CO

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaoning Normal University, Dalian 116029, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaoning Normal University, Dalian 116029, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shantou University, Shantou 515063, P.R. China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shantou University, Shantou 515063, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The integration of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with g-C3N4/NiAl-LDH heterostructure significantly strengthens harvestability of light irradiation and transfer efficiency of electrons, and thus giving rise to highly enhanced photocatalytic activity of CO2-to-CO in water under simulated sunlight.

A series of treble NiAl-LDH/g-C3N4/carbon quantum dots (LDH/CN/CQDs) photocatalysts is successfully prepared for the photoreduction of CO2 to CO via a facile hydrothermal pathway. In the 3D flower-like LDH/CN/CQDs, CQDs not only achieve the efficient inhibition of charge recombination but also act as the unhindered “electronic bridges” to synergistically construct a classical type-Ⅱ charge transfer configuration, which synchronously permits the effluence of photogenerated electrons from CN to LDH and holes from LDH to CN, and promotes ultraviolet–visible irradiation respondence. The sample of LDH/CN/CQDs-6 is the optimal one amongst the LDH/CN/CQDs with a larger special surface area (98.43 m2g−1) and an appropriate content of CQDs (66.9 wt%), exhibiting the highest CO evolution rate (5.2 μmol·g−1·h−1) under visible light irradiation without any sacrificial agent or photosensitizer in water. This is 26.8- and 20.9-fold higher than those of the pristine LDH, pure CN, and their binary counterparts, respectively, and also outperforms most reported LDH-based photocatalysts. As unhindered electron conduction bridges, the highly dispersed CQDs in the LDH/CN heterojunction significantly increase utilization efficiency of light energy and separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. This work provides a beneficial attempt to integrate CQDs with LDH/CN for the positive synergetic effect on both photoelectric properties and electron transfer to obtain highly enhanced photocatalytic activity of CO2 into CO, and expected to be extended towards broader photocatalytic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The recycling of carbon dioxide (CO2) is one of the most critical problems for humans to maintain healthy environment. As a potential carbon resource from the perspective of green chemistry, the conversion of CO2 into high value-added hydrocarbon fuels (such as HCOOH, CO, and CH3OH) has received increasing attention over the past years. Amongst existing feasible conversion technologies, photocatalysis provides unique advantages as follows: It is nontoxic and harmless to the environment; it uses inexhaustible energy source supplied by solar energy; its reaction conditions are milder and more controllable than those of thermal catalysis and electrocatalytic conversion. Therefore, exploring and developing photocatalysts with high activity is the main task for efficient CO2 conversion.

Amongst numerous developed semiconductor photocatalysts, graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) exhibits non-negligible merits, including a controllable electronic configuration caused by a conjugated structure similar to the graphene structure, suitable band gap for light harvest of medium and long wavelength bands, benign thermodynamical and structural stability, low preparation cost, and nontoxicity to human [1–4]. However, bulk g-C3N4 still presents few shortcomings in application, such as a poor specific surface area (SBET), a low efficiency of photon usage, and the fast recombination of photogenerated charges. To overcome these, some attempts have been made to construct heterostructures between g-C3N4 and semiconductors. In these attempts, layered double hydroxide (LDH) has attracted considerable interests due to its structural and qualitative individualism. Most LDHs are formulated as [M1 1- x M2 x (OH)2 ]q+Ax/n
n
–·y H2O, where M1 denotes a monovalent (Li+) or divalent cation (Fe2+, Co2+,Cu2+, Ni2+, etc.) and M2 represents a trivalent (Ce3+, Ga3+,Cr3+, In3+, Mn3+) or tetravalent (Ti4+, Sn4+, Zr4+) cation [5–8]. For instance, Wang et al. prepare CoAl-LDH nanosheets by employing a simple co-precipitation method. These nanosheets exhibit merits of the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 into CH4 (under atmospheric CO2 concentration, 4.3 μmolg-1h−1) without any sacrificial electron donor or noble mental cation [9]. Because of the abundant of hydroxide ions in the structure of LDH, it shows splendid performance to capture CO2 molecules. Thus, LDH and g-C3N4 have been combined into a new type of LDH/g-C3N4 (LDH/CN) composite photocatalyst with the dual advantages of them [10–13]. S. Tonda et al report that the NiAl-LDH/CN composite not only enhances the adsorption capacity of CO2 on the catalyst surface but also accelerates the transport of photogenerated carriers without electron donors or other additives, which induce remarkably enhanced CO2 reduction into CO and H2 under visible light irradiation, far superior to that of either single phase CN or NiAl-LDH [10]. J. Liu et al. find that the ZnCr, FeNi, and MgAl-LDH/CN composite can improve the reduction activity of CO2 mainly due to the special internal electron transport structure (type-Ⅱ or Z scheme) and the efficacious separation and transmission of photogenerated carriers under light irradiation [11–13]. Although the reported LDH/CNs can enhance CO2 absorption capability and increase charge separation efficiency, their poor surface redox potential and weak light harvestability impede the improvement of CO2 reduction activity and hydrocarbon fuel selectivity. Therefore, searching for a suitable helper to further improve the photoelectric properties of LDH/CN composite is highly desirable. In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been extensively utilized in photocatalytic studies due to their following traits: a) From the structural level, CQDs belong to zero-dimensional nanomaterials with a particle size of < 5 nm. b) From the optical electronic level, CQDs are a type of up-conversion photoluminescent material, which can quickly realize the separation of electrons and inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs. c) From the preparation perspective, many synthesis methods are very simple [14–17]. In addition, the CQDs surface has abundant surface functional groups, which can firmly nucleate and anchor onto semiconductor nanocrystals due to robust electrostatic interactions [18–22]. D. Tang et al. synthesize various CQDs-doped and -modified semiconductor photocatalysts (CQD/BiVO4, CQD/Cu2O, CQD/Ag3PO4, etc.), which can prompt the light response ability compared with their pristine counterparts [23–26]. M. Zhang et al. introduce CQDs into MgAl-LDH to construct a binary semiconductor heterojunction by utilizing the multi-group nature of CQDs surface to enhance the adsorption capacity for anionic organic dyes [27]. Therefore, it is reasonable to infer that targeted integration of LDH/CN with CQDs would boost the photoelectric properties, and thus giving rise to highly enhanced photocatalytic activity.

With the research basis and anticipation as motivation, herein, we directionally synthesize a novel NiAl-LDH/g-C3N4/CQDs (LDH/CN/CQDs) photocatalyst by a simple yet effective hydrothermal method. As expected, the obtained LDH/CN/CQDs exhibits highly enhanced photocatalytic performance for CO2 reduction to CO, compared with the pristine NiAl-LDH and pure g-C3N4, binary LDH/CN, LDH/CQDs, and CN/CQDs, respectively. The possible type-Ⅱ charge transfer paths with structure–activity relationship on the enhanced photocatalytic activity are surveyed and discussed in depth.

Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, AR grade), aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (AR grade), melamine (AR grade), ammonium fluoride (AR grade), oleic acid (AR grade), polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP, AR grade) and nickel nitrate hexahydrate (AR grade) were purchased from ALADDIN Reagent Co., Ltd. Urea (AR grade) and citric acid (AR grade) were supplied by Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company.

The g-C3N4 was prepared through a conventional calcination. Specifically, 2.0 g of melamine was dissolved in 80 mL of DMSO and vigorously stirred to obtain a clear solution A. Similarly, a clear solution B was obtained by dissolving 2.04 g of cyanuric acid in 40 mL of DMSO. Then, the solution A and B were mixed until a white precipitate was completely formed. After drying overnight, the as-obtained precipitate was carefully transferred into several alumina combustion boats. Then, heating treatment was conducted in a muffle furnace at 550 °C (4 h, 2.3 °C/min). A brownish g-C3N4 powder (hereafter denoted as CN) was obtained.

CQDs were prepared through a facile hydrothermal synthesis. Typically, 1.20 g of urea was added to 24 mL of deionized water with 1.05 g of citric acid. When the solution became clear, it was transferred into a Teflon autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. Finally, most of water present in the dialyzed solution was evaporated, and then 20 mL of black solution was left to obtain liquid CQDs.

For NiAl-LDH/CN, 0.09 g of Al(NO3)3·9H2O, 0.22 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water with 0.30 g of urea and 0.07 g of NH4F for 1 h to obtain a solution C. Subsequently, solution D was obtained by ultrasonically dispersing 0.16 g of CN into 20 mL of deionized water for 30 min. Afterwards, solution C and D were rapidly mixed with 1 h of consecutive churning, and then, the mixture was quickly transferred into a 100-mL Teflon autoclave (120 °C, overnight). Finally, the obtained precipitate (hereafter denoted as LDH/CN) was collected and washed several times with an appropriate quantity of deionized water and ethanol.

The synthesis procedure of LDH/CN/CQDs was similar to that for the LDH/CN. The as-prepared LDH/CN/CQDs with the same amount of LDH/CN powder and CQDs are referred to as LDH/CN/CQDs-X (X = 2, 6, 10, and 14), where X is the amount of dropwise added CQDs (in millilitres). After calculation, the CQDs contents in the LDH/CN/CQDs-X were 40.2, 66.9, 77.1, and 82.5 wt%, respectively. Pristine LDH was synthesized utilizing one-step hydrothermal treatment without the addition of CN or CQDs. For comparison, LDH/CQDs (6 mL CQDs on LDH) and CQDs/CN (6 mL CQDs on CN) were synthesized with the identical process.

Various techniques used for sample characterisation are listed in Supporting information . These techniques include powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS), scanning electron microscope (SEM), N2-sorption, ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS), X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra, transient photocurrent, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay spectra, and the Mott-Schottky plots.

Photocatalytic activities of CO2 photoreduction of the as-prepared samples as photocatalysts were evaluated in a 200-mL quartz reactor with a quartz window to receive successive light irradiated atop it. The reactor was thoroughly brimmed with CO2 or N2 for 15 min, and the reaction temperature was maintained at 15 °C, 20 °C and 25 °C, respectively, by using an external cooling circulation unit. Briefly, 20 mg of the photocatalyst was completely dispersed in 100 mL of deionized water with constant stirring under irradiation from a 300 W Xe-lamp (ProfectLight, PLS-SXE300C). During irradiation, 5 mL of the inner gas of quartz reactor was pulled out and detected 4 times with the interval of one hour by using a gas chromatograph (GC-5890, Nanjing kejie instrument with a barrier ionization detector). Isotopic labelling test with the only input gas of 13CO2 was performed to determine the carbon source in the products by an assembly of mass spectrometry and gas chromatography (MS-5975 and GC-7920). The selectivity of CO and H2 was evaluated in line with the following.

are the yields of CO and H2, respectively. The apparent quantum yield (AQY) of CO2 photoreduction was measured at a wavelength of 370 nm and calculated by the following formula:

where NCO, NA, h, c, and λ denote the amount of CO (mol), Avogadro’s constant (6.02 × 1023 mol−1), Planck constant (6.62 × 10-34 J·s), the speed of light (3.0 × 108 m·s−1), and the wavelength (m), respectively.

XRD is used to obtain the crystal structure of the samples, as shown in Fig. 1
a and 1b. For the pristine LDH, the reflection peaks appearing at 11.62°, 23.38°, 35.02°, 39.4°, 46.96°, 61.01°, and 62.48° are orderly indexed to the (003), (006), (012), (015), (018), (110), and (113) lattice planes of hexagonal NiAl-LDH (JCPDS No. 22–0452). Two obvious diffraction peaks appearing at approximately 27.5° (002) and 12.7° (100), corresponding to the peculiarly stacked conjugated aromatic rings and inner periodically repeated-stacking segment, respectively, are present in all composites comprising the CN monomer [28]. Superimposition reflection peaks of LDH and CN in both LDH/CN and LDH/CN/CQDs-X are observed, indicating that the introduction of CQDs does not destroy the lattice structure. Another phenomenon observed for LDH/CN/CQDs-X is the similar diffraction peaks subtly weaken and shift towards the lower angles, compared with the peaks of pristine LDH and pure CN, which indicates that lattice spacing between layers reduces and lattice expands [29]. FT-IR is performed for further investigating the chemical structures of the samples. As shown in Fig. 1 c and 1d, the pristine LDH exhibits three segmental characteristic peaks, corresponding to the asymmetrical stretching vibration of NO3 – within the lamellar unit (1365 cm−1), OH bending-stretching vibration pattern (3480 and 1635 cm−1), and the translational vibrations of metal–oxygen and metal–oxygen-metal bridge bands below 800 cm−1, respectively [30–32]. For the pure CN, several peaks ranging from 1200 to 1650 cm−1 are derived from the stretching vibrations of conjugated aromatic heterocycles, and the sharp peak (805 cm−1) results from the characteristic breathing mode of the tri-s-triazine ring system [28]. The characteristic peaks of CN and LDH can be identified in both LDH/CN/CQDs-X and LDH/CN, indicating the successful synthesis of LDH and CN structures. Compared with those of LDH/CN, all the LDH/CN/CQDs not only maintains all the characteristic peaks of LDH and CN but also exhibits a slight red-shift after the addition of CQDs (Fig. 1 d). This phenomenon can also verify CQDs is introduced into inter-galleries.

The morphologies and microstructures of the samples are observed through SEM, TEM, and HR-TEM, respectively. As illustrated with the good correlation of SEM images with TEM ones, the pristine LDH shows a type of 3D flower-like morphology, which constitutes a large amount of ultra-thin nanoflakes ( Fig. S1
a, 2a, and 2b), while pure CN exhibits typical sheet-like structure with serious stacking ( Fig. S1
b and 2c). The LDH/CN and LDH/CN/CQDs-6 have almost the same appearance with catkin-like pore channels, that is, LDH integrates with CN surface in the form of lamella staking under in situ growth. However, CQDs cannot be observed in the SEM image of LDH/CN/CQDs-6, which is attributed to its very small sizes (Fig. 2
c and 2d). The LDH/CN/CQDs-6 exhibits two lattices in accordance to the interplanar distances of 0.21 and 0.26 nm (Fig. 2 e), which are indexed to the (012) plane of LDH and (100) plane of CQDs [33,34], respectively. The interplanar distance of pure CQDs is approximately 0.21 nm ( Fig. S2
a). These findings reveal successful combination of treble materials. The EDS mapping-images present a highly-dispersed mode of C, N, O, Ni, and Al elements in the LDH/CN/CQDs-6 rather than presenting as a simple physical mixture (stacked discontinuously segment) of constitutive LDH, CN, and CQDs with the aggregation state of corresponding elements ( Fig. S3 ).Fig. 3. .

N2-sorption isotherms with relevant pore size distributions are employed to investigate the relation between the surface area of the as-prepared samples and CO2 photoreduction capacity. As shown in Fig. S4
, classical type-Ⅱ isotherms and nearly-H4 hysteresis loop are observed for all the samples with a certain porous configuration of approximately 25 nm (Table S1). These samples have hysteresis loops, but no obvious adsorption platforms are observed near P/P0 = 1, which indicated they contain both mesopores and macropores. The SBET values of LDH/CN/CQDs-X increase strikingly after the introduction of CQDs, which possibly results from the effect of various functional groups on CQDs, which changes the microenvironment for the nucleation and growth of LDH when it is integrating with the as-prepared CN. As a result, more porosity is formed in the resulting LDH/CN/CQDs-X, compared with the LDH, CN, and LDH/CN ( Fig. S2
b and Table S1). In any case, the increments of SBET on LDH/CN/CQDs-X endow more active sites for harvesting light and CO2 adsorption, thereby potentially favourable for a higher photoreduction activity.

The XPS spectra are utilized to assign the surface elemental compositions and detailed chemical status of the samples. As shown in Fig. S5
and Fig. 3., the XPS spectrum of LDH/CN/CQDs-6 with the relative peaks of C, N, Ni, and Al elements reveals the presence of pristine LDH and CN composites. For comparison, those relative peaks can be also indexed in LDH/CN, further indicating the successful integration of LDH, CN, and CQDs. Two peaks (284.8 and 288.1 eV), ascribed to the presence of extraneous or graphitic C (CC/CC) coordination and NC-N units in the tri-s-triazine ring, can be fitted in the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of CN [35]. The peaks of the graphitic C component of LDH/CN/CQDs-6 are considerably higher than those of CN or LDH/CN, which implies that CQDs are introduced into the heterojunction. For pristine CN, the N 1 s spectrum exhibits three main peaks, corresponding to the binding energies after deconvolution, at 401, 399.8, and 398.4 eV, assigned to amino functional groups (NH), bridged tertiary nitrogen N-(C)3 groups of three tri-s-triazine moieties, and sp2 hybridised nitrogen of triazine rings (CN-C) within a tri-s-triazine hetero ring, respectively [29,36]. In the high-resolution Ni 2p spectrum, two peaks of LDH (856.2 and 873.8 eV) are assigned to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, which is consistent with the theoretical Ni2+ oxidation state. Additional satellite peaks indicate the presence of the high-spin divalent Ni in the samples [37,38]. In C 1 s and N 1 s spectra, the peaks of LDH/CN shift to a low binding energy of approximately 0.35 eV, and those of LDH/CN/CQDs-6 slightly equidirectionally move compared with those of CN, which may be due to changes in the bonding composition. The content of ionic bonds in LDH/CN is higher than that in pure CN, and that of covalent bonds in treble LDH/CN/CQDs-6 is higher than that in LDH/CN due to the introduction of the surface group in CQDs. Moreover, the presence of Al3+ oxidation state is observed for LDH, LDH/CN, and LDH/CN/CQDs-6 in the high-resolution Al 2p spectrum. Both the results of the Ni 2p and Al 2p peak height reduction indicate a decrease in the content ratio of nickel and aluminium ions with the introduction of CQDs and/or CN into the reaction system. The blue- and red-shift binding energies of Ni 2p/Al 2 s and C 1 S/N 1 s suggest the strong electron transfer effect between the surface of CN and LDH through CQDs [39,40].

UV–vis DRS is conducted to obtain the irradiation harvestability of the samples. As shown in Fig. 4
a, three main absorption bands of LDH are located at 200–300, 300–500, and 500–800 nm, respectively. The involuted adsorption edge at 200–300 nm is related to the ligand–metal charge migration (LM) from the O 2p orbital to Ni 3d t2g orbital within LDH frameworks. Other bands located in near the infrared and visible regions are indexed to the d-d transitions of Ni (II) species in the octahedral field. Spin-allowed transitions (3A2g(F) → 3T1g(P) and 3A2g(F) → 3T1g(F)) are stemmed from Ni2+ ions (d8 configuration) in an octahedral realm near 400 and 700 nm. The band gap between the two peaks at 450–600 nm is ascribed to the spin-forbidden transitions of 3A2g(F) → 1T2g(D) and 3A2g(F) → 1Eg(D). A slight red shift is observed when CQDs are introduced into the LDH/CN photocatalyst, which indicates that the presence of CQDs promotes the irradiation harvest capacity. The UV–vis DRS of blank CQDs is also performed with the π → π* transitions of aromatic sp2 CC species (236 nm) and n → π* transitions of CO bonds (334 nm) ( Fig. S2
c). Upgraded irradiation respondence towards complete spectrum with the aid of CQDs indicates that the synergistic effect among LDH, CN, and CQDs materials can be used to transfer photogenerated carriers and to substantially increase the efficiency of light utilization. Derived from UV–vis DRS and the classical Tauc method, the calculated band gaps (Eg) for CN, LDH, LDH/CN, and LDH/CN/CQDs-6 are 2.26, 2.35, 2.31, and 2.19 eV, respectively (Fig. 4 b). The optimized narrow band gap leads to the transfer of more photogenerated carriers, which is favourable for photocatalytic reactions.

Driven by simulated sunlight, photocatalytic CO2 reduction activities of the samples as photocatalysts are evaluated under atmospheric temperature (20 °C) without any sacrificial agent or photosensitiser, during which CO is the main product with negligible trace amount of CH4. At the same time, the evolution amounts of H2 and O2 are also detected because the reduction reaction competes with the water splitting reaction. As shown in Fig. 5
a, both pristine LDH and pure CN have the poor activities after 4 h reaction with very low CO yields of 0.77 and 0.99 μmol·g−1, respectively. By contrast, the binary counterparts display improved CO yields: LDH/CN for 2.28 μmol·g−1, LDH/CQDs for 1.84 μmol·g−1, and CN/CQDs for 2.61 μmol·g−1, respectively. After further integration of the LDH, CN, and CQDs, the resulting LDH/CN/CQDs-X exhibit significantly enhanced CO yields up to the highest value of 20.75 μmol·g−1 for LDH/CN/CQDs-6 corresponding a CO evolution rate of 5.2 μmol·g−1·h−1, which is 26.8, 20.9, 10.7, 9.0, 11.2, and 8.0 times higher than those of the LDH, CN, CQDs, LDH/CN, LDH/CQDs, and CN/CQDs, respectively, and even superior to many reported LDH-based photocatalysts (Table S2). This is originated from strong synergistic effect of the constitutive LDH, CN, and CQDs. At a wavelength of 370 nm, the AQY is measured and calculated to be 0.18%. Furthermore, the LDH/CN/CQDs-6 has an O2 evolution rate of 2.7 μmol·g−1·h−1 with a stoichiometric ratio of CO to O2 of ∼ 1.93, which is close to the theoretical value of 2 ( Fig. S6 ). The corresponding H2 evolution rate is as low as 0.75 μmol·g−1·h−1, verifying that most photogenerated electrons over the valence band (VB) of LDH/CN/CQDs-6 participate in CO2 reduction instead of water splitting. The selectivity of CO and H2 are 87.4% and 12.6%, respectively. Addition of too much CQDs (LDH/CN/CQDs-10 and LDH/CN/CQDs-14) cause the congestion of CQDs and overwhelming surface coverage of LDH and CN, which decreases the light utilization and amount of active sites, and thus deteriorating CO yields. After six consecutive cycles, the CO yield slight drops to 14.73 μmol·g−1 over the LDH/CN/CQDs-6, and its XRD pattern, FT-IR spectra, and TEM image do not change significantly, indicating that the LDH/CN/CQDs-6 has good cycling photostability (Fig. 5 b and S7). To check that if the CO origin is from the CO2 photoredcution, a series of comparative experiments with varied reaction condition is performed, the results are listed in Table S3. In the absence of one of the three conditions (light, CO2, and H2O), the CO yield is almost negligible, implying that these conditions are indispensable to CO2 photoreduction. In the isotope analysis for carbon source, three peaks at m/z = 29, 32 and 45 are detected in the mass spectrometry and ascribed to 13CO from 13CO2, O2 from H2O oxidation, and the excessive 13CO2, respectively (Fig. 5 c). This is a potent evident that CO is sourced from CO2 photoreduction but not from self-degradation, and O2 is formed by holes oxidation of H2O. In addition, CO2 thermal reduction can induce CO2 reduction. To clarify the effect of it, CO2 photoreduction evaluations are also probed at two different temperatures of 15 °C and 25 °C, respectively. With the increasing temperature, the CO yield increases slightly from 5.0 to 5.5 μmol·g−1·h−1, demonstrating the positive thermal contribution to CO2 photoreduction (Fig. 5 d).

Fig. 6 presents the various photoelectric properties of different samples. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the LDH/CN/CQDs-6 delivers the highest and stable photocurrent of ∼ 3.22 × 10-6 A, which is 26.8, 5.3, 2.6, and 2.4 times higher than those of CN, LDH, LDH/CN, and CN/CQDs, respectively. Similarly, its EIS has the smallest fitted-arc radius amongst the samples (Fig. 6 b). In addition, the PL intensity of LDH/CN/CQDs-6 is weaker than those of LDH, CN, and LDH/CN (Fig. 6 c), and its charge carrier lifetime (τ) fitted from the TRPL decay spectra is 1.91 ns, less than those of LDH (2.18 ns), CN (2.12 ns), and LDH/CN (2.13 ns) (Fig. 6 d and Table S4), indicating a lower charge transfer resistance it has. All of the results reveal that the LDH/CN/CQDs-6 possesses the higher separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [41–43]. This is likely due to the bridging role of CQDs, which sets up numerous smooth channels for migration of interfacial charge between the heterostructure of LDH and CN.

Mott-Schottky plots are measured to obtain the flat-band potentials of LDH (-0.80 V) and CN (-1.25 V) under three different frequencies (1.5, 2.0, and 2.5 KHz). As shown in Fig. S8 , both LDH and CN belong to n-type semiconductors with the positive relationship of the Mott-Schottky plots, meaning that their conduction band (CB) potentials are approaching to the flat potential. Based on the conventional conversion formula from SCE to NHE: ENHE = EAg/AgCl – 0.197 + 0.059 pH, the CB potentials (ECB) of LDH and CN are formulated to be −0.58 and −1.03 V (vs NHE), respectively. In combination the determined Eg values of LDH (2.35 eV) and CN (2.26 eV) from UV–vis DRS and the empirical equation of Eg = EVB - ECB, where the EVB is the valence potential, the EVB values of the two semiconductors are therefore converted to be 1.77 and 1.23 eV, respectively, Because the ECB values of LDH and CN are more negative than that of the theoretical reduction potentials of CO2-to-CO (-0.53 eV), a type-II heterojunction mechanism for CO2 photoreduction over the LDH/CN/CQDs-6 is schematically proposed under light irradiation, as illustrated in Fig. 7 . After light irradiation, the photoexcited electrons can migrate from the VB of both LDH and CN to their corresponding CB with the same amount of holes left in the VB. The wider Eg value of LDH leads to a lower amount of photogenerated electrons excited than that in CN. Then, the local electric field at the interface of LDH/CN heterojunction pushes the simultaneous transfer of photogenerated electrons from the CB of CN to that of LDH with the assistance of CQDs and holes from the VB of LDH to that of CN·H2O are oxidized to generate O2 and protons by holes (+0.83 eV) at the VB of CN. These greatly suppress the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. As a quantum dots material, the highly dispersed CQDs in the LDH/CN heterojunction not only significantly enhance light absorption capacity with higher utilization efficiency of light energy, but also act as numerous smooth electron-conduction bridges to further facilitate the electron transfer. In addition, the strikingly increased SBET of the LDH/CN/CQDs-6 (98.4 m2 g−1) provides more usable reactive sites. All of the above merits ultimately result in highly enhanced photocatalytic reduction activity for CO2-to-CO.

In summary, a novel 3D flower-like treble LDH/CN/CQDs photocatalyst is successfully prepared through facile hydrothermal synthesis. CQDs with microcosmic sizes of 3 to 4 nm are well dispersed on the LDH/CN surface with tight junctions. Such highly-dispersed CQDs convey photogenerated charge carriers between interactive heterojunctions and inhibit recombination and efficaciously aid the separation of electron-hole pairs. The photocatalytic activities of LDH/CN on CO2 reduction under complete spectrum irradiation are boosted substantially by using CQDs without any sacrificial reagent or photosensitiser. The optimal sample of LDH/CN/CQDs-6 exhibits a high CO yield of 20.67 μmol·g−1 (5.2 μmol·g−1·h−1) at a reaction time of 4 h, which is much higher than those of the pristine CN, pure LDH, their binary counterparts. A type-II heterojunction mechanism for charge transfer is proposed accordingly. This study provides a meritorious demonstration for designing CQDs-assisted LDH-based heterojunctions for enhanced activity of CO2 photoreduction.

Wentao Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Qiu Wang: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Zhi Liu: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Guixiang Ding: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21871124), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province, China (2021A1515010071), and the Start-up Grant for Talents of Shantou University (130-09419024).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.04.102.

ce:section.ce:para:
